analogy: Think of "Development" through the chapter's own opening images: NOTES FOR TEACHERSNOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER I : DEVELOPMENT Development has many aspects. The purpose of this chapter is to enable students to understand this idea. They have to understand that people have different perspectives on development and there are ways by which we... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: NOTES FOR TEACHERSNOTES FOR THE TEACHER, CHAPTER I : DEVELOPMENT, DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT, CHAPTER I. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Development is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that nOTES FOR TEACHERSNOTES FOR THE TEACHER CHAPTER I : DEVELOPMENT Development has many aspects.

The text explains that the purpose of this chapter is to enable students to understand this idea.

The text explains that they have to understand that people have different perspectives on development and there are ways by which we can arrive at common indicators for development.

The text explains that to do this, we have used situations that they can respond to in an intuitive manner; we have also presented analysis that is more complex and macro in nature.

The text explains that how can countries or states be compared using some selected development indicators is another question that students would read about in this chapter.

The text explains that economic development can be measured and income is the most common method for measuring development.

The text explains that however, the income method, though useful, has several weaknesses.

The text explains that hence, we need newer ways of looking at development using indicators of quality of life and environmental sustainability.

The text explains that it is necessary for you to expect the students to respond actively in the classroom and on a topic such as the above, there would be wide variation in opinion and possibility of debate.

The text explains that at the end of each section there are a few questions and activities.

The text explains that these serve two purposes: first, they recap the ideas discussed in the section and second, they enable better understanding of the themes discussed by bringing the learners closer to their real life situations.

The text explains that there are certain terms used in this chapter that would require clarification — Per Capita Income, Literacy Rate, Infant Mortality Rate, Attendance Ratio, Life Expectancy, Gross Enrolment Ratio, and Human Development Index.

The text explains that though data pertaining to these terms are provided, these would need further explanation.

The text explains that you may also need to clarify the concept of Purchasing Power Parity that is used to calculate Gross National Income Per Capita in Table 1.6.

The text explains that it is necessary to keep in mind that these terms are used as an aid to the discussion and not something to be memorised.

The text explains that sources for Information The data for this chapter is taken from reports published by the Government of India (Economic Survey, Reports of the National Family Health Survey and Handbook of Statistics on the Indian Economy), United Nations Development Programme (Human Development Report) and World Bank (World Development Indicators).

The text explains that development involves thinking about these questions and about the ways in which we can work towards achieving these goals.

The text explains that this is a complex task and in this chapter we shall make a beginning at understanding development.

The text explains that also, you will find answers to many of these questions not just in economics but also in your course in history and political science.

The text explains that in the same way, it is only through a democratic political process that these hopes and possibilities can be achieved in real life.

The text explains that dEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT DEVELOPMENT CHAPTER I “Without me they cannot develop... in this system I cannot develop!”

The text explains that explain some of the important ideas of the above section in your own words.

The text explains that it is true that if women are engaged in paid work, their dignity in the household and society increases.

The text explains that most likely, you would find that different students in the class have given different answers to the above question.

The text explains that national development means thinking about these questions.

The text explains that read this newspaper report and answer the questions that follow: LET’S WORK THESE OUT If even the idea of what constitutes development can be varied and conflicting, then certainly there can be differences about ways of developing.

The text explains that before we come to this, let us consider another question.

The text explains that from this, can we conclude that all sections of the economy have become better?

The text explains that source : Economic Survey 2024-25, Statistical Appendix, p.

The text explains that 32 State Literacy Rate % Net Attendance Ratio (per 2017–18 100 persons) secondary stage (aged 15–17 years) 2017–18 Haryana 28 82 73 Kerala 6 94 94 Bihar 27 62 69 Sources : Economic Survey 2024–25, National Sample Survey Organisation (Report No.

The text explains that 585), National Statistical Office, Government of India; National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5) 2019-21 IIPS, Mumbai.

The text explains that the reason is — money in your pocket cannot buy all the goods and services that you may need to live well.

The text explains that so you are actually able to study because many other children also want to study and because many people believe that the government should open schools and provide other facilities so that all children have a chance to study.

The text explains that even now, in many areas, children, particularly girls, are not able to go to high school because the government/society has not provided adequate facilities.

The text explains that in many other states also we are nowhere near realisation of the constitutional goal of free and compulsory education for all children up to the age of 14, which was expected to be achieved by 1960.

The text explains that does availability of good health and educational facilities depend only on amount of money spent by the government on these facilities?

The text explains that similarly, in some states, the Public Distribution System (PDS) functions well.

The text explains that in another 25 years, 60 per cent of the country would be doing the same if the present way of using this resource continues.

The text explains that groundwater overuse is particularly found in the agriculturally prosperous regions of Punjab and Western U.P., hard rock plateau areas of central and south India, some coastal areas and the rapidly growing urban settlements.” (a) Why groundwater is overused?

The text explains that d D D D DE V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T 15 15 15 15 15 Groundwater is an example of renewable resources.

The text explains that these resources are replenished by nature as in the case of crops and plants.

The text explains that however, even these resources may be overused.

The text explains that for example, in the case of groundwater, if we use more than what is being replenished by rain then we would be overusing this resource.

The text explains that fOR EXAMPLE, CRUDE OIL THAT WE EXTRACT FROM THE EARTH IS A NON- RENEWABLE RESOURCE.

The text explains that hOWEVER WE MAY FIND A SOURCE OF OIL THAT WE DID NOT KNOW OF EARLIER.

The text explains that example 2: Example 2: Example 2: Example 2: Example 2: Exhaustion of Exhaustion of Exhaustion of Exhaustion of Exhaustion of Natural Resources Natural Resources Natural Resources Natural Resources Natural Resources Look at the following data for crude oil.

The text explains that list a few examples of environmental degradation that you may have observed around you.

The text explains that for each of the items given in Table 1.6, find out which country is at the top and which is at the bottom.

The text explains that the following table shows the proportion of adults (aged 15-49 years) whose BMI is below normal (BMI <18.5 kg/m2) in India.

The text explains that it is based on a survey of various states for the year 2019-21.

The text explains that state Male Female (%) (%) Kerala 8.5 10 Karnataka 17 21 Madhya Pradesh 28 28 All States 20 23 D D D D DE V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T E V E L O P M E N T 17 17 17 17 17 (i) Compare the nutritional level of people in Kerala and Madhya Pradesh.

The text explains that (ii) Can you guess why around one-fifth of people in the country are undernourished even though it is argued that there is enough food in the country?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Understanding Economic Development, Chapter 1: Development

Do not add outside examples to Development; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use NOTES FOR TEACHERSNOTES FOR THE TEACHER and CHAPTER I : DEVELOPMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
